title: Probing sliding ferroelectricity in bilayer T$_\mathrm{d}$-WTe$_2$ with high-harmonic generation
authors: Elias Greil, Alba de las Heras, Angel Rubio, Anna Galler
abstract: High-harmonic generation is a sensitive all-optical probe of symmetry and electron dynamics in solids. Here, we use first-principles time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) to study high-harmonic generation in T$_d$-WTe$_2$, a two-dimensional semimetal with switchable out-of-plane ferroelectric polarization driven by interlayer sliding. We show that the mirror-symmetry breaking underlying the ferroelectric state produces robust signatures in polarization-resolved high-harmonic spectra, enabling optical identification of the polarization state. By incorporating interlayer shear motion in coupled electron-lattice TDDFT simulations, we further show that the 0.24 THz shear mode is slow enough to remain effectively decoupled from the ultrafast electronic response responsible for harmonic emission. Our results establish high-harmonic spectroscopy as a non-invasive probe of sliding ferroelectricity and lattice symmetry in two-dimensional quantum materials.
summary: This paper demonstrates that high-harmonic generation can be used as a powerful optical tool to detect ferroelectricity in 2D materials. By studying bilayer WTe2, the authors show that the breaking of specific crystal symmetries during interlayer sliding leads to unique z-polarized harmonic signals. The study uses advanced TDDFT simulations to prove that these electronic signatures are robust and decoupled from slow lattice vibrations. This provides a non-invasive way to monitor the switching of ferroelectric states in quantum materials.
main_problem: Investigating whether high-harmonic generation (HHG) can serve as an all-optical, non-invasive probe to identify and monitor sliding ferroelectricity and lattice symmetry in 2D materials.
main_result: The breaking of glide-plane symmetry in the ferroelectric phase of bilayer WTe2 produces robust, detectable z-polarized harmonics, providing a spectroscopic fingerprint for the ferroelectric state.
method: First-principles simulations using time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) and molecular dynamics (MD-TDDFT) implemented in the Octopus code, combined with Jones matrix formalism for symmetry analysis.
model_or_system: Bilayer Td-WTe2, a 2D semimetal featuring switchable out-of-plane ferroelectric polarization driven by interlayer sliding/shear motion.
key_observables: Polarization-resolved high-harmonic spectra (x, y, and z components) and the emergence of even vs. odd harmonic orders.
important_parameters: 800 nm NIR pulse, 16-cycle duration, 1 TW/cm2 peak intensity, and a 0.24 THz interlayer shear phonon mode.
main_assumptions: The Ehrenfest approximation is used for electron-ion coupling; the translational component of the glide-plane operation is ignored in symmetry analysis; spin-orbit coupling and spin polarization are neglected.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the THz pump-NIR probe setup and the energy profile of the switching pathway; Figure 2 compares HHG spectra for different phases; Figure 7 compares MD-TDDFT with fixed-ion simulations; Figure 8 illustrates symmetry-driven selection rules.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of probing sliding ferroelectricity, describes the structural and symmetry properties of WTe2, details the TDDFT/MD methodology, presents the results of polarization-resolved HHG spectra, and concludes with the utility of HHG as a diagnostic tool.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic of the THz pump - NIR probe setup for ferroelectric switching. (a) A THz pump pulse excites coherent phonons in bilayer Td-WTe2, fol- lowed by a time-delayed NIR pulse, which drives HHG to probe the lattice and electronic structure. (b) Dynam- ics of the 0.24 THz interlayer shear phonon. (c) Poten- tial energy as a function of interlayer shear displacement, calculated using the nudged elastic band (NEB) method within DFT (see Methods). The THz pulse can drive a transition from the FE Td phase to a non-ferroelectric (NFE) intermediate state.17

FIG. 2: Signatures of sliding ferroelectricity in HHG. (a) The polar Td structure of bilayer WTe2 exhibits FE polarization in the out-of-plane (z) direction. (b) HHG spectrum obtained for a 16-cycle driving laser pulse with a wavelength of 800 nm, linearly polarized along the x direction, and an intensity of 1 TW/cm2. (c) Corresponding HHG spectrum for a driving laser polarized along the y direction. (d–f) Same as in (a–c), but for the non-ferroelectric (NFE) intermediate structure, obtained from Td via an interlayer shear displacement along y. The z-polarized harmonics (shaded in grey) are strongly suppressed. (g–i) For a full comparison, we also show the HHG spectra from monolayer WTe2.

FIG. 4: Symmetries of the non-ferroelectric 1T′(∗) structure. (a) Mirror plane Mx (corresponding to the yz plane). (b) Glide-plane symmetry Gz, consist- ing of a reflection across the xy plane combined with a fractional translation along the y direction.

FIG. 3: HHG response along the FE switching pathway. (a) Harmonic yield of the z-polarized harmon- ics as a function of the shear displacement ∆y. The HHG yield is computed for an x-polarized driving field with a wavelength of 800 nm and an intensity of 1 TW/cm2. The z harmonics progressively vanish as the shear dis- placement approaches the NFE 1T′(∗) structure. (b) The ferroelectric polarization is directly proportional to the deviation from the xy glide-plane symmetry, which is present only in the 1T′(∗) structure. The deviation from this symmetry is quantified by a mean absolute displace- ment measure D (see Methods).
FIG. 5: Symmetries of the ferroelectric Td struc- ture. (a) Mirror plane Mx. (b) Illustration of the broken glide-plane symmetry Gz.
FIG. 6: Symmetries of monolayer WTe2. (a) xy- plane of the monolayer highlighting the mirror symmetry Mx. (b) The monolayer additionally shows a two-fold rotational symmetry C2(x) around the x-axis.

FIG. 7: Results of the MD-TDDFT simulations. (a) Comparison of the total HHG spectrum obtained from coupled electron–ion dynamics (MD-TDDFT, red) with that from fixed ionic positions corresponding to the Td phase of bilayer WTe2 (TDDFT, blue). (b) Time evo- lution of the ionic displacements during the first 350 fs fol- lowing initialization of the interlayer shear mode. Shown are the displacements ∆y of the centers of mass of the top layer (dashed black line) and bottom layer (solid black line). After 300 fs, a 16-cycle 800 nm probe pulse is ap- plied, and the resulting HHG spectrum is shown in (a).

FIG. 8: Shared symmetries of the NFE 1T′(∗) structure and an x-polarized external field. (a) The glide-plane symmetry Gz leaves both the crystal structure and the external field invariant. (b) The mirror symmetry Mx maps the external field onto itself with a temporal shift of T0/2, corresponding to a reversal of its direction.
